title: How We Learn: The Surprising Truth About When, Where, and Why It Happens
author: Benedict Carey
excerpt: A lively, accessible tour of learning science that reveals why many of our intuitions about studying are wrong—and why forgetting, messing up, and sleeping are actually essential to mastering new material.
keyIdeas: Forgetting is not a failure of memory but an essential feature of how memory works
The spacing effect is one of the most robust findings in all of learning science
Testing yourself is far more effective than re-reading
Sleep plays an active role in memory consolidation
keyTakeaways: Allow yourself to forget—it's part of the learning process
Space your study sessions rather than massing them
Test yourself frequently; it's not just assessment but learning itself
Get enough sleep; your brain consolidates learning during rest
whoShouldRead: Students looking to study more effectively
Anyone curious about how memory actually works
Teachers and parents helping others learn
Readers who enjoyed Make It Stick and want a more narrative treatment
whoShouldSkip: Those who want highly prescriptive study formulas
Readers who prefer dense academic citations to storytelling
